content.text: I need a function that can extract the content following existing delimiters within a string up to a specified cursor position.

This solution first determines the effective substring based on whether a `cursor_pos` is provided or not. It then uses an internal regex object (`self._delim_re`) to split this specific portion of the line and returns the last element found after splitting.

```python
def split_line(self, line, cursor_pos=None):
        """Split a line of text with a cursor at the given position.
        """
        l = line if cursor_pos is None else line[:cursor_pos]
        return self._delim_re.split(l)[-1]
```

The function relies on `self._delim_re`, so its behavior (what constitutes a delimiter) depends entirely on how that Regex object is initialized. Using index slicing (`line[:cursor_pos]`) correctly ensures splits only happen in the requested area of the text.
If there are multiple delimiters, will this function return all parts after each one or just the final segment?
It returns only the final segment (`[-1]`). If you need every part that follows a delimiter up to that position, you would iterate through the results of `self._delim_re.split(l)` instead.
That makes perfect sense for limiting context scope.
